query: evidence that pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) is an approved method to prevent HIV infection

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Prophylaxis (Archived) -- Issues of Concern -- Pre-Exposure Prophylaxis (PrEP)
retrieved_docs.content: Background

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Prophylaxis (Archived) -- Issues of Concern -- Pre-Exposure Prophylaxis (PrEP)
retrieved_docs.content: In 2015, about 1.1 million people aged 13 and older in the United States were living with HIV. The number of new HIV diagnoses in the United States in 2015 was 38,500, which has declined since 2010. Although rates are falling overall, rates of new HIV diagnoses in specific subpopulations are not following this trend. Black men (especially males having sex with males-MSM) have shown no change, and rates among Hispanic MSM are increasing.

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Prophylaxis (Archived) -- Issues of Concern -- Pre-Exposure Prophylaxis (PrEP)
retrieved_docs.content: HIV seems to affect specific subsets of the population disproportionately. In 2015, 67% of newly diagnosed HIV cases were among MSM. 55% of new diagnoses were among adolescents and young adults (age 13 to 34), and 44% of new diagnoses were among blacks.

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Prophylaxis (Archived) -- Issues of Concern -- Pre-Exposure Prophylaxis (PrEP)
retrieved_docs.content: It developed PrEP to help reduce the number of new HIV diagnoses. PrEP therapy includes tenofovir-emtricitabine, a single pill taken daily to prevent HIV infection. Multiple clinical studies have shown this regimen to be useful for PrEP. Overall, when taken as prescribed, PrEP has been found PrEP to be over 90% effective in preventing HIV infection. Based on that, the FDA approved the use of tenofovir-emtricitabine for PrEP in 2012, and in 2014, PrEP clinical practice guidelines were developed by the CDC. Indications Physicians show PrEP in high-risk populations. High-Risk MSM An HIV-negative man with having anal sex without condoms (receptive or insertive) within the past 6 months A bacterial STI (syphilis, gonorrhea, or chlamydia) was diagnosed or reported within the past 6 months. Heterosexually Active Men and Women

retrieved_docs.title: Preexposure Prophylaxis for HIV Prevention -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: PrEP for HIV is an essential part of clinical HIV prevention and health maintenance. The rationale for PrEP, epidemiology of HIV transmission in high-risk populations, the identification of those at risk, treatment options, monitoring of PrEP, management of complications, client support for adherence to therapy, and individual, programmatic, and systems methods to enhance healthcare team outcomes in providing PrEP to clients are integral to HIV prevention. Using quality improvement and public health lenses ensures maximal benefit to those most at risk.

retrieved_docs.title: Preexposure Prophylaxis for HIV Prevention -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Preexposure prophylaxis of HIV (PrEP) is an essential component of the recommended package for HIV prevention, according to the World Health Organization (WHO) and United States Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC). [3] [4] Preexposure prophylaxis (PrEP) involves the intake of antiretroviral medications either continuously or for identified high-risk events and is highly effective in preventing HIV. Compared to placebo, consistent use of daily oral PrEP reduces the risk of sexual transmission of HIV by up to 92%. [5] PrEP with bimonthly injectable cabotegravir lowers the chance of sexual transmission of HIV by about 70% compared to those taking tenofovir disiproxil fumarate/emtricitabine (TDF/FTC). [6] [CDC. PrEP Guidelines. 2021] Though one trial of PWID observed individuals 40 years or older, those who used methamphetamines, and those with recent incarcerations had lower adherence than those without these risk factors, other studies have indicated the efficacy and cost-effectiveness of PrEP in preventing HIV in this population. [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Preexposure Prophylaxis for HIV Prevention -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Laboratory-based HIV antigen-antibody test: Use this if the decision to start PrEP was based on a rapid or point-of-care HIV test.

retrieved_docs.title: Preexposure Prophylaxis for HIV Prevention -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: DEXA (dual-emission x-ray absorption) scans for bone density are not recommended to initiate or monitor oral PrEP. For appropriate assessment and management, referral should be made for those with a history of fragility fractures or significant risk of fractures.[CDC. PrEP Guidelines. 2021] See the section on "Treatment" below for further information on the timing of initiation of therapy and required monitoring while on PrEP.

retrieved_docs.title: Preexposure Prophylaxis for HIV Prevention -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: (See StatPearls' companion topic, " HIV Prevention ," for further information on modes of transmission of HIV, pharmaceutical strategies for at-risk populations, implementation considerations for select populations, and select public health measures of relevance across the interdisciplinary team.)

retrieved_docs.title: Preexposure Prophylaxis for HIV Prevention -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Sexually transmitted infection testing: Test using updated local guidelines for recommended assays; testing for syphilis, chlamydia, and gonorrhea should be included.

retrieved_docs.title: Preexposure Prophylaxis for HIV Prevention -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: This activity provides clinicians with vital knowledge to tackle the ongoing global challenge of HIV prevention. Exploring the rationale, treatment options, and adherence strategies for PrEP empowers participants with the insights needed to address the unique needs of diverse populations and deliver optimal care to individuals at significant risk of HIV infection. Clinicians learn strategies for effective care coordination and communication among team members, ensuring an approach beyond medication prescription. Empowering clinicians involves integrating quality improvement and public health perspectives, emphasizing the significance of local epidemiology and the social contexts influencing HIV transmission. This approach enables the development of tailored services, programs, and policies that effectively address the population's needs.

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Prevention -- Clinical Significance -- Preexposure Prophylaxis
retrieved_docs.content: The choice of PrEP regime should be based on a discussion between the person and provider and based on the person's risk factor profile, comorbid conditions, preference for oral or injectable medications, and ability to take medications reliably, in addition to the adverse effect profile and cost of the medication if not covered through insurance or pharmaceutical-sponsored programs. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Preexposure Prophylaxis for HIV Prevention -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Renal function: Test renal function if oral PrEP is being considered due to the risk of proximal renal tubular disease associated with long-term TDF use.

retrieved_docs.title: Preexposure Prophylaxis for HIV Prevention -- Epidemiology -- Demand for and Use of Preexposure Prophylaxis
retrieved_docs.content: Overall, progress in HIV treatment and prevention in priority groups has stalled between 2010 and 2022—and has regressed in some areas. While those in sex occupations and their clients have experienced decreases in infections over this period, in some regions, MSM, transgender individuals, and people who inject drugs have not benefited equally from HIV prevention efforts. Globally, PrEP use among people from key populations remains very low, often under 5%.[UNAIDS. Global Report. 2023] This can be due to structural barriers from lack of awareness, availability of medications, or affordability to individual beliefs that they are not at risk or not a candidate for PrEP.

retrieved_docs.title: Preexposure Prophylaxis for HIV Prevention -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Hepatitis C serology: This is only for MSM, transgender women, and people who inject drugs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 135
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is strongly associated with shared needle use, which constitutes a major transmission route, and its prevention and treatment rely on antiretroviral agents classified by mechanism of action. Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) target the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme, mimic natural nucleosides, and cause chain termination, thereby inhibiting viral DNA synthesis. Key NRTIs include tenofovir disoproxil, abacavir, didanosine (dideoxycytidine), emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, and zidovudine; each has demonstrated activity in pre‑exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) and in early or combination treatment regimens to suppress viral replication. The non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor delavirdine mesylate also possesses preventive properties by directly inhibiting reverse transcriptase. Integrase inhibitors—cabotegravir, dolutegravir, elvitegravir, and raltegravir—block the viral integrase enzyme, preventing integration of viral DNA into the host genome; cabotegravir is notable for its dual use in both treatment of established infection and PrEP, while the other agents are primarily employed for treatment but may be used preventively in high‑risk settings. Collectively, the correct classification and combined use of NRTIs, non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors, and integrase inhibitors form the backbone of modern HIV prevention strategies and therapeutic protocols, optimizing patient outcomes through viral suppression and reduced acquisition risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2665
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is closely linked to the practice of shared needle use, which serves as a significant route of transmission. Antiretroviral agents such as delavirdin‑mesylate, efavirenz, and etravirine have been identified as medications that may prevent HIV infection, highlighting their potential role in pre‑exposure prophylaxis and broader infection‑control strategies. Understanding both the behavioral risk factor of shared needle use and the preventive capabilities of these drugs is essential for effective patient counseling, risk reduction, and public‑health interventions aimed at curbing the spread of HIV.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2086
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is closely linked to the practice of shared needle use, which serves as a significant route of transmission. The antiretroviral agents delavirdin‑mesilát and elvitegravir are both recognized for their potential to prevent HIV infection, highlighting their role in pre‑exposure prophylaxis and broader strategies to reduce the risk associated with behaviors such as shared needle use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 54
retrieved_docs.content: Enfuvirtid, delavirdin‑mesilát, dolutegravir, elvitegravir and raltegravir are antiretroviral agents that may prevent HIV infection, indicating a role in pre‑exposure prophylaxis. Cabotegravir and fostemsavir are antiretroviral drugs that may be used to treat HIV infection, providing therapeutic options for individuals with established disease. Dolutegravir, elvitegravir, cabotegravir and raltegravir are classified as members of the Integrase Inhibitors, anti‑infectives for systematic use, reflecting their common mechanism of inhibiting the HIV integrase enzyme. HIV infection is associated with the shared use of needles, underscoring the importance of harm‑reduction strategies to prevent transmission. Together, these relationships highlight both preventive and treatment modalities within the integrase inhibitor class and emphasize a key behavioral risk factor for HIV acquisition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 153
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is strongly associated with shared needle use, a principal route of transmission, highlighting the need for behavioral risk reduction alongside pharmacologic interventions. A broad spectrum of antiretroviral agents is employed for both pre‑exposure prophylaxis and treatment. Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) that prevent HIV replication include lamivudin, emtricitabin, zidovudin, stavudin, tenofovir disoproxil and dideoxycytidine. Non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) comprise delavirdin, delavirdin‑mesilát, efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapin and rilpivirin, all classified within the NNRTI group. Integrase strand‑transfer inhibitors—dolutegravir, elvitegravir, raltegravir and cabotegravir—contribute to prevention, and cabotegravir is also indicated for treatment of established HIV infection. An important safety consideration is the contraindicated interaction between pantoprazole and rilpivirin, which mandates avoidance of their co‑administration. Collectively, these relationships delineate the major antiretroviral drug classes, their individual members, and critical drug‑drug interaction information essential for effective and safe HIV management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 1214
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is closely linked to the practice of shared needle use, highlighting a major route of transmission that requires targeted harm‑reduction strategies. Pharmacologic agents such as delavirdin‑mesilát, lamivudin, and zidovudin have each been identified as having preventive potential against HIV infection, underscoring the role of antiretroviral medications in pre‑exposure prophylaxis and infection control. Integrating needle‑sharing risk mitigation with the use of these preventive drugs is essential for reducing new HIV cases and improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 65
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is closely linked to the practice of shared needle use, which represents a major route of transmission. A variety of antiretroviral agents are recognized for their ability to prevent acquisition of HIV, including the non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) delavirdine, delavirdine‑mesylate, efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine, rilpivirine, and doravirine, as well as the integrase inhibitors dolutegravir, raltegravir, and cabotegravir. Among these, cabotegravir is also noted for its therapeutic role in treating established HIV infection. Delavirdine, efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine, rilpivirine, and doravirine are all members of the class “Non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors,” while dolutegravir, cabotegravir, and raltegravir belong to the class “Integrase Inhibitors, antiinfectives for systematic use.” Doravirine carries a specific contraindication with the antimicrobial rifapentine, indicating that co‑administration should be avoided. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how both NNRTIs and integrase inhibitors contribute to HIV prevention strategies—such as pre‑exposure prophylaxis—and, in the case of cabotegravir, to ongoing treatment regimens, underscoring the importance of class‑based drug selection and awareness of drug‑specific contraindications in comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 0 -- Community 9
retrieved_docs.content: molecules. Adjunctive agents include the antibiotic aztreonam (1000 mg injectable) and the cholesterol‑binding resin cholestyramine (4000 mg oral suspension). Pre‑exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) for HIV utilizes lamivudine, nevirapine, raltegravir, rilpivirine, stavudine and zidovudine, while established infection is treated with integrase inhibitors, entry inhibitors and other agents. A clinically important drug‑drug interaction is the contraindication of the proton‑pump inhibitor pantoprazole with the NNRTI rilpivirine, underscoring the need for medication reconciliation. Collectively, these classifications, specific agents, mechanisms of action, and safety relationships enable clinicians to tailor antiviral, antifungal, antibacterial and adjunctive therapies for HCV, HIV, influenza, ocular viral infections, smallpox, COVID‑19, and related complications such as bacterial pneumonia following influenza, thereby optimizing individualized patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 148
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is strongly associated with shared needle use, a major route of transmission, making harm‑reduction strategies such as avoiding needle sharing essential for prevention. Prophylactic and therapeutic management relies on antiretroviral agents, including the non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) delavirdin, delavirdin‑mesilát, efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapin and rilpivirin, and the integrase strand transfer inhibitors (INSTIs) dolutegravir, elvitegravir, raltegravir and cabotegravir. Cabotegravir is notable for both pre‑exposure prophylactic activity and treatment of established HIV infection. All listed NNRTIs and INSTIs are administered systemically. Clinicians must also consider drug‑interaction contraindications; pantoprazol should not be co‑administered with rilpivirin.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1414
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is a viral disease that is contraindicated with the use of methotrexate sodium, indicating that patients with HIV should not be treated with this immunosuppressive medication. The condition is closely associated with the practice of shared needle use, a major route of transmission that underscores the importance of harm‑reduction strategies in prevention. Antiretroviral agents such as delavirdin‑mesylate and elvitegravir have been identified as having the potential to prevent HIV infection, highlighting their role in pre‑exposure prophylaxis and early intervention efforts. Understanding these relationships—avoidance of methotrexate sodium in HIV‑positive individuals, the risk posed by needle sharing, and the preventive capabilities of delavirdin‑mesylate and elvitegravir—is essential for optimizing patient care and reducing new infections.

retrieved_docs.content: belonging to the nucleosides/nucleotides excl. RT‑inhibitors class) and idoxuridine (derived from deoxyuridine). Vidarabin also includes fludarabine, linking this agent to the same class. A large subset of antivirals is used for HIV pre‑exposure prophylaxis or other preventive regimens, including efavirenz, emtricitabine, etravirine, ibalizumab, lamivudine, nevirapine, raltegravir, rilpivirine, stavudine, zidovudine, enfuvirtide, abacavir, delavirdine, delavirdine‑mesylate and dolutegravir. Cabotegravir and fostemsavir are employed to treat established HIV infection. NNRTIs (efavirenz, delavirdine, rilpivirine) and NRTIs (tenofovir disoproxil) illustrate distinct mechanisms targeting reverse transcription, whereas integrase inhibitors (cabotegravir, dolutegravir, raltegravir) block viral integration. Rilpivirine is contraindicated with pantoprazole, and methotrexate sodium is contraindicated in patients with HIV infection. Needle sharing is identified as a risk factor for HIV

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1880
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is closely associated with the practice of shared needle use (společné užívání jehel), highlighting a key route of transmission that is important for prevention strategies. The antiretroviral agent delavirdin‑mesylate has been reported to have a preventive effect against HIV infection, indicating its potential role in pre‑exposure prophylaxis. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1229

Antiretroviral agents such as delavirdin‑mesilát and etravirine have demonstrated the ability to prevent HIV infection, highlighting their potential role in pre‑exposure prophylaxis and broader infection‑control strategies. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2031
retrieved_docs.content: Shared needle use (společné užívání jehel) is a recognized route of transmission that increases the risk of acquiring HIV infection. The antiretroviral agents delavirdin‑mesylate and dolutegravir have both been shown to have preventive activity against HIV infection, serving as options for pre‑exposure prophylaxis or early treatment to block viral acquisition. Recognizing the connection between needle sharing and HIV infection highlights the need for harm‑reduction interventions alongside the availability of preventive pharmacotherapies such as delavirdin‑mesylate and dolutegravir to reduce new infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 33
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is strongly associated with shared‑needle use, which constitutes a major transmission route. A broad spectrum of antiretroviral agents is employed for both pre‑exposure prophylaxis and treatment of established infection. Preventive and therapeutic drugs reported include delavirdin, delavirdin‑mesylate, dolutegravir, efavirenz, emtricitabine, etravirine, lamivudine, nevirapine, raltegravir, stavudine, zidovudine, tenofovir disoproxil, cabotegravir and dideoxycytidine. Delavirdin, efavirenz, etravirine and nevirapine belong to the non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NNRTI) class, which binds to a distinct site on the viral reverse transcriptase enzyme. Emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, zidovudine, tenofovir disoproxil and dideoxycytidine constitute the nucleoside/nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NRTI) class, acting as substrate analogues that terminate DNA synthesis. Cabotegravir, dolutegravir and raltegravir are integrase inhibitors that block viral DNA integration into the host genome, with cabotegravir highlighted for its therapeutic efficacy. The complementary mechanisms of NNRTIs and NRTIs—different binding sites on reverse transcriptase—enable the construction of combination regimens that maximize viral suppression, limit resistance development, and support comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1141
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is strongly linked to the practice of shared needle use, which constitutes a major route of transmission. A variety of antiretroviral agents are recognized for their roles in either preventing acquisition of HIV or treating established infection. Delavirdin, its mesylate salt (delavirdin‑mesilát), efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine, raltegravir, and rilpivirine are all reported to have preventive activity against HIV infection, reflecting their use in pre‑exposure prophylaxis or post‑exposure settings. Cabotegravir is noted for its therapeutic potential in managing HIV infection, and may also be employed in long‑acting prophylactic regimens. These drugs belong to two principal pharmacologic classes. Cabotegravir, dolutegravir, and raltegravir are members of the Integrase Inhibitors (anti‑infectives for systematic use) class, which target the viral integrase enzyme to block integration of viral DNA into the host genome. Delavirdin, delavirdin‑mesilát, efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine, and rilpivirine are classified as Non‑nucleoside Reverse Transcriptase Inhibitors (NNRTIs), which bind to reverse transcriptase at a site distinct from nucleoside analogues and inhibit viral RNA‑dependent DNA synthesis. Understanding the transmission risk associated with shared needle use and the preventive or therapeutic functions of these integrase inhibitors and NNRTIs is essential for comprehensive HIV prevention strategies and effective patient care.
